content: For what value of $a, 6$ lies between the roots of the equation $x^2+2(a-3) x+9=0$.
explanation:
Let $f(x)=x^2+2(a-3) x+9$.

If 6 lies between the roots of $f(x)=0$, then we must have the following conditions:

(i) Disc $>0$ and (ii) $f(6)<0$

[ $\because$ coefficient of $x^2$ is positive]

Now (i) Disc $>0$

$$ \begin{array}{lc} \Rightarrow & 4(a-3)^2-36>0 \\ \Rightarrow & (a-3)^2-9>0 \\ \Rightarrow & a^2-6 a>0 \\ \Rightarrow & a<0 \text { or } a>6\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \\ \text { and Eq. (ii) } & f(6)<0 \\ \Rightarrow & 36+12(a-3)+9<0 \\ \Rightarrow & 12 a+9<0 \\ \Rightarrow & a<-\frac{3}{4} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{array} $$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

                               $$ a<-\frac{3}{4} $$

i.e.                                $$ a \in\left(-\infty, \frac{-3}{4}\right) $$

content: Roots of the equation $a x^2+b x+c=0(a, b, c>0)$ are

options.content: depends on the values of $a, b$ and $c$

explanation:
For the quadratic equation, $a x^2+b x+c=0$, using the quadratic formula

$$ x=\frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4 a c}}{2 a} $$

Sum of roots $=-\frac{b}{a}$

Since, $a, b>0$, so $-\frac{b}{a}$ is negative, meaning the sum of the roots in negative.

Products of roots $=c / a$

Since, $a, c>0$, this means the product is positive we know that if the product of roots is positive and the sum of roots is negative, both roots must be negative.

content: Roots of the equation $ x^{2}+b x-c=0(b, c > 0) $ are
explanation:
To determine the nature of the roots of the equation $ x^2 + bx - c = 0 $ (where $ b, c > 0 $), we need to consider the sign of the product of the roots.

Key Points:

If the roots are of the same sign:

The product of the roots is positive.

If the roots are of opposite signs:

The product of the roots is negative.

Let's analyze the given equation $ x^2 + bx - c = 0 $:

The product of the roots for the quadratic equation $ ax^2 + bx + c = 0 $ is given by $ \frac{c}{a} $.

For our equation:

The product of the roots is $ \frac{-c}{1} = -c $.

Since $ c > 0 $, the product $-c$ is negative.

Conclusion:

The roots of the equation $ x^2 + bx - c = 0 $ must be of opposite signs, as the product of the roots is negative.

content: Number of real solution of $ \sqrt{5-\log _{2}|x|} $ $ =3-\log _{2}|x| $ is equal to
explanation:
Let $ \log _{2}|x|=t \quad \ldots \text { (i) } $

$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \sqrt{5-t}=3-t \\ \Rightarrow & 5-t=(3-t)^{2} \\ \Rightarrow & 5-t=9+t^{2}-6 t \end{array} $

$ \begin{array}{rrr} \Rightarrow & t^{2}-5 t+4 =0 \\ \Rightarrow & (t-4)(t-1) =0 \\ \Rightarrow & t =1 \text { or } 4 \text { [rejected] } \end{array} $

$ [\because \sqrt{5-4}=3-4,1 \neq-1] $

$ \begin{array}{lr} \Rightarrow & \log _{2}|x|=1 \\ \Rightarrow & |x|=2 \\ \Rightarrow & x= \pm 2 \end{array} $

$ \therefore $ Total 2 real solutions.

content: If $$\alpha<1$$ be a root of the equation $$2 x^2-5 x+2=0$$, then the other root of the equation is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } 2 x^2-5 x+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 x^2-4 x-x+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 x(x-2)-1(x-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(2 x-1)(x-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{1}{2}, 2 \quad \therefore \quad \alpha=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \text { Now, } 4 \alpha^2+4 \alpha=4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=1+2=3 \neq 2 \\ & 4 \alpha^3-3 \alpha=4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3-3\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2}=-1 \neq 2 \\ & 4 \alpha^2-2 \alpha=4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2-2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=1-1=0 \neq 2 \\ & 4 \alpha^3+3 \alpha=4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3+3\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{3}{2}=2 \end{aligned}$$

Thus, other root be $$4 \alpha^3+3 \alpha$$.

content: Let $$\alpha, \beta$$ be the roots of the equation $$x^2-p x+r=0$$ and $$\frac{\alpha}{2}, 2 \beta$$ be the roots of the equation $$x^2-q x+r=0$$. Then, the value of $$r$$ is equal to
explanation:
Given $$\alpha, \beta$$ are the roots of $$x^2-p x+r=0$$

$$\therefore \quad \alpha+\beta=p, \alpha \beta=r$$

$$\because \quad \frac{\alpha}{2}, 2 \beta$$ are roots of $$x^2-q x+r=0$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{\alpha}{2}+2 \beta=q, \alpha \beta=r$$

Now, $$\alpha+\beta=p$$ and $$\alpha+4 \beta=2 q$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \beta & =\frac{2 q-p}{3} \text { and } \alpha=p-\left(\frac{2 q-p}{3}\right) \\ & =\frac{2(2 p-q)}{3} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Hence, } r=\alpha \beta & =\left(\frac{2}{3}(2 p-q)\right)\left(\frac{2 q-p}{3}\right) \\ & =\frac{2}{9}(2 p-q)(2 q-p) \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\alpha$$ be a root of the equation $$4{x^2} + 2x - 1 = 0$$, then the other root of the equation is
explanation:
$$4{x^2} + 2x - 1 = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\alpha + \beta = - {1 \over 2},\alpha \beta = - {1 \over 4}$$ ..... (i)

Also, $$4{\alpha ^2} + 2\alpha - 1 = 0$$ as $$\alpha$$ is a root and we have to prove that $$\beta = 4{\alpha ^3} - 3\alpha $$.

Now, $$4{\alpha ^3} - 3\alpha = 4{\alpha ^2}\,.\,\alpha - 3\alpha $$

$$ = \alpha (1 - 2\alpha ) - 3\alpha = - 2\alpha - 2\alpha $$

$$ = - {1 \over 2}[4{\alpha ^2} + 4\alpha ) = - {1 \over 2}[1 - 2\alpha + 4\alpha ]$$

$$ = - {1 \over 2}(1 + 2\alpha ) = - {1 \over 2} - \alpha = \beta $$

Now, $$\alpha + \beta = - {1 \over 2}$$ [from Eq. (i)]

Hence, $$\beta = 4{\alpha ^3} - 3\alpha $$.

content: Let a, b be the solutions of x2 + px + 1 = 0 and c, d be the solution of x2 + qx + 1 = 0. If (a $$-$$ c) (b $$-$$ c) and (a + d)(b + d) are the solution of x2 + ax + $$\beta$$ = 0, then $$\beta$$ is equal to
explanation:
Since, a + b = $$-$$p, ab = 1 ..... (i)

and c + d = $$-$$q, cd = 1

Now, (a $$-$$ c) (b $$-$$ c) and (a + d) (b + d) are the roots of x2 + ax + $$\beta$$ = 0

(a $$-$$ c) (b $$-$$ c) (a + d) (b + d) = $$\beta$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ (ab $$-$$ ac $$-$$ bc + c2) (ab + ad + bd + d2) = $$\beta$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ {1 $$-$$ c (a + b) + c2} {1 + d(a + b) + d2} = $$\beta$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ (1 + pc + c2)(1 $$-$$ pd + d2) = $$\beta$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ 1 $$-$$ pd + d2 + pc $$-$$ p2cd + pcd2 + c2 $$-$$ pc2d + c2d2 = $$\beta$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ 1 $$-$$ pd + d2 + pc $$-$$ p2 + pd + c2 $$-$$ pc + 1 = $$\beta$$ [$$\because$$ cd = 1]

$$\Rightarrow$$ 2 + d2 + c2 $$-$$ p2 = $$\beta$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ 2cd + c2 + d2 $$-$$ p2 = $$\beta$$

(c + d)2 $$-$$ p2 = $$\beta$$

q2 $$-$$ p2 = $$\beta$$ [$$\because$$ (c + d) = $$-$$ q]

content: If a$$\in$$R, b$$\in$$R, then the equation x2 $$-$$ abx $$-$$ a2 = 0 has

options.content: one positive root and one negative root

explanation: Let $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ be the roots of x2 $$-$$ abx $$-$$ a2 = 0 where, $$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$ = ab and $$\alpha$$$$\beta$$ = $$-$$a2, which shows product of roots < 0, i.e. one root must be negative and the other must be positive. Hence, equation has one positive root and one negative root.

content: The solution of the inequality $${4^{ - x + 0.5}} - {7.2^{ - x}} < 4$$, x $$\in$$R is
explanation:
$${4^{ - x + 0.5}} - {7.2^{ - x}} < 4$$

Let $${2^{ - x}} = t$$

The equation becomes

$$2{t^2} - 7t - 4 < 0 \Rightarrow (t - 4)(2t + 1) < 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2(t - 4)\left\{ {t - \left( { - {1 \over 2}} \right)} \right\} < 0 \Rightarrow - {1 \over 2} < t < 4$$

Since, $$t = {2^{ - x}} > 0\forall x \in R$$

$$ \Rightarrow 0 < t < 4 \Rightarrow 0 < {2^{ - x}} < {2^2}$$

As, 2x is an increasing function,

$$-$$x < 2 or x > $$-$$2

Solution is $$( - 2,\infty )$$.

content: Let x1 and x2 be the real roots of the equation $${x^2} - (k - 2)x + ({k^2} + 3k + 5) = 0$$, then maximum value of $$x_1^2 + x_2^2$$ is
When x100 is divided by x2 $$-$$ 3x + 2, the remainder is (2k + 1 $$-$$ 1)x $$-$$(2k $$-$$ 1), then k is
explanation:
Let f(x) = x100

g(x) = x2 $$-$$ 3x + 2

q(x) = quotient

r(x) = remainder = (ax + b)

$$\therefore$$ f(x) = g(x) q(x) + r(x)

x100 = (x2 $$-$$ 3x + 2) q(x) + ax + b

Put x = 1 $$\to$$ 1 = a + b ..... (i)

Put x = 2 $$\Rightarrow$$ 2100 = 2a + b ..... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

a = 2100 $$-$$ 1, b = 2 $$-$$ 2100

Remainder = (2100 $$-$$ 1)x + (2 $$-$$ 2100)

= (299 + 1 $$-$$ 1)x $$-$$ (299 $$-$$ 1)

$$\therefore$$ k = 99
